nativeRange.habitat: Found across a range of reef habitats in the western Indo-Pacific, from shallow lagoons to reef slopes to approximately 30 m depth. Derives its specific epithet from Lord Howe Island, the southernmost Pacific coral reef.

decorPreferences: stable base with spacing from neighboring corals

diet.notes: Primarily photosynthetic via zooxanthellae. Has been documented benefiting from target (spot) feeding once or twice per week with meaty frozen foods such as mysis shrimp, brine shrimp, cyclops, and small zooplankton. Feeding tentacles generally extend at night; target feeding via pipette or turkey baster is commonly advised.

avoidWith: corals placed within sweeper tentacle range (~5–7 cm)
aggressive LPS corals without buffer spacing
other Micromussa colonies without 10+ cm buffer

summary: Micromussa lordhowensis (Aussie Lord) is a beginner-to-intermediate LPS coral reclassified from Acanthastrea lordhowensis in 2016. Prized for vivid multi-color morphs; generally tolerant, benefits from weekly spot feeding and moderate lighting.
careNotes:
Taxonomy note — 2016 Arrigoni et al. reclassification: Acanthastrea lordhowensis (Veron & Pichon, 1982) was reclassified to Micromussa lordhowensis by Arrigoni et al. in 2016, based on morphological and molecular phylogenetic analysis showing it was not closely related to the genus type Acanthastrea echinata but was instead closely related to other Micromussa species. The genus Micromussa now belongs to family Lobophylliidae (Order Scleractinia). The trade name 'Acan Lord' and 'Aussie Lord' persist in the hobby and refer exclusively to this species post-reclassification. Cross-reference: [acanthastrea-coral] (sw-coral-034), which documents A. echinata (the 'true Acan') — that entry remains a distinct species and is no longer grouped with Micromussa lordhowensis. The genus Acanthastrea was reduced to approximately eight species following the reclassification.

Note on M. amakusensis: Micromussa amakusensis (Veron, 1990) is a second commonly traded species in the genus; it has a similar distribution and overlapping care requirements, and is sometimes offered under the same 'Micromussa' or 'Acan' trade umbrella. It tends to have smaller corallites than M. lordhowensis.

Key care notes:
• Lighting: Moderate PAR is generally advised — commonly cited around 75–200 PAR. ReefChasers specifically recommends a primary target of 50–100 PAR with tolerance up to 200 PAR for acclimated specimens. Excessive light has been reported to damage tissue; acclimate new specimens by placing lower in the tank and gradually moving to the desired position over 2–4 weeks.
• Flow: Low to moderate, indirect flow is preferred. Direct high flow has been reported to cause polyp retraction or tissue tears. Gentle alternating or random flow that keeps the colony clean without stressing tissue is commonly recommended.
• Placement: Lower to middle areas of the aquarium are generally advised, away from direct current impact and high-intensity light zones.
• Aggression: Sweeper tentacles extend approximately 5–7 cm (2–3 inches) at night and are capable of stinging neighboring corals. A minimum buffer of 10 cm from other specimens is commonly advised, with more space as the colony grows.
• Feeding: Target (spot) feeding once or twice per week is generally considered beneficial, with meaty frozen foods such as mysis shrimp delivered via pipette. The species has been documented as an aggressive feeder with polyps that actively accept presented food.
• Colony size: Wild colonies have been documented forming low clumps up to two metres across. In home aquaria, typical colonies grow to 5–50 cm depending on tank size and care. Individual corallites measure approximately 8–15 mm in diameter.
• Care level disagreement: LiveAquaria rates this coral 'Easy' (beginner), while ReefChasers rates it 'Moderate'. The intermediate designation here reflects the average of these two assessments; many hobbyists find it one of the more forgiving LPS corals once basic reef stability is established.
• Temperature disagreement: LiveAquaria reports 72–78°F (22–26°C); ReefChasers reports 77–84°F (25–29°C). The averaged range of 24–27°C is used here. The upper bound discrepancy suggests the species can tolerate warmer water than some sources state; reviewer should verify against Australian reef habitat data.
• Colors: Wild specimens are frequently dull gray or brown; aquaculture and trade specimens commonly exhibit vivid multi-colored polyps (reds, oranges, purples, blues, greens) due to selective breeding and collection of colorful morphs. Designer color morphs command premium prices in the trade.
• Fragging: Colonies can be fragged with bone cutters or a bandsaw. Frags readily encrust and grow on frag plugs or rubble. Fragging is the primary propagation method in captivity; the species is described as fast-growing relative to many other LPS corals.
breedingNotes: Fragging (cutting) is the primary propagation method in captivity. Colonies can be fragged with bone cutters or a bandsaw; frags readily encrust on frag plugs or rubble. The species has been described as 'fast growing' relative to other LPS corals in ideal conditions. Sexual reproduction occurs via broadcast spawning on natural reefs; deliberate captive sexual breeding is not commonly practiced in the hobby.

additional.name: ReefChasers – Micromussa lordhowensis Coral Care Guide
additional.notes: Cross-checked temperature (77–84°F vs LiveAquaria 72–78°F — averaged to 24–27°C), lighting PAR (50–100 primary, up to 200 acclimated), calcium (400–450 ppm), alkalinity (8–9 dKH), care level (Moderate vs LiveAquaria Easy — averaged to intermediate), and sweeper tentacle reach (2–3 inches / 5–7.5 cm). Temperature ranges disagree significantly; averaged value used, flagged in careNotes.

additional.name: Wikipedia – Micromussa lordhowensis
additional.notes: Used for taxonomy (Order: Scleractinia, Family: Lobophylliidae), native range (western Indo-Pacific: Red Sea through East China Sea, Japan, Australia), depth range (to ~30 m), colony size (low clumps up to 2 m across), corallite diameter (to 15 mm), and confirmation of 2016 Arrigoni et al. reclassification from Acanthastrea lordhowensis. Not used for husbandry parameters.

additional.name: Wikipedia – Micromussa (genus)
additional.notes: Used for genus-level taxonomy confirmation (Family Lobophylliidae, Order Scleractinia) and species list. Confirmed M. lordhowensis and M. amakusensis as the two primary traded species. LiveAquaria listed family as 'Mussidae' (outdated pre-2016 classification); Lobophylliidae adopted per Wikipedia and current WoRMS taxonomy.
